次に、「難波江」は「難波浦」・「難波潟」と同じ地形を示すことから、これらは同じ地域を示す名称と見なせる。その一方で、「難波浦」の位置については『住吉大社神代記』（年不詳・8世紀前半頃？）に「從三國川尻至吾君川尻難波浦」・「爰三韓國調貢從此川運進。」とある。また、『百練抄』と『栄華物語』には、後三条院たちが岩清水社・住吉社・四天王寺を周遊した時の記事がある。このうち、『百練抄』延久五年（1073年）二月条には「（前略）廿二日 覧難波浦。廿五日 覧長柄橋。（以下、略）」と、また『栄華物語』「松のしづえ」延久五年（1073年）条では、（二月）廿二日に長柄橋から中津川（現在の淀川）へ、そして廿五日には御幣島にかけて周遊したとある。御幣島は、現在の大阪市西淀川区御幣島のことであるから、少なくとも「難波浦」（＝難波江）とは三国川河口一帯すなわち現在の神崎川下流域に比定される。
以上から、難波津は上町台地から三国川河口の沖合を挟んだ位置、つまり大阪市淀川区・豊中市・尼崎市南部一帯がその候補地となる。
さらに、遣唐使船の出発地を抽出すると「難波江口（江口）」、「三津」、「難波津」の3カ所が挙げられる。「三津」については、『続日本紀』天平勝宝五年（753年）九月条の「摂津國御津村南風大吹。」の「御津村」と混同されるが、遣唐使船あるいは外国使節船との関係で現れる地名に「御津」の表記はない。よって、「三津（浦・浜）」と「御津」は区別でき、前者は大阪市淀川区三津屋付近となる可能性があることから、大阪市側に難波津の位置を求める根拠は乏しい。また、天平勝宝8年（756年）に作成された『摂津職河辺郡猪名所地図』に「大物濱」「長洲濱」「杭瀬濱」という海浜にかかる記述があるにもかかわらず、「難波津」はみられないので、尼崎市域も候補地から除外される。残る豊中市南部については、三国川分流と考えられる2条以上の旧河道が確認されているが、その一つは現在の上津島・名神口付近で古代の猪名川と合流すると推定される。その合流点付近には、弥生時代から平安時代にかけての流通拠点と位置づけられている利倉南遺跡・利倉西遺跡・上津島遺跡・上津島南遺跡・島田遺跡・庄内遺跡（以後、これらの遺跡を「上津島遺跡群」と呼ぶ。）が展開している。
以上、各候補地の状況をふまえると、難波津の位置は豊中市南部の上津島・名神口に展開する上津島遺跡群に求めるのが、最も合理的である。
== 関連文献 == * 北川建次ほか編『瀬戸内海事典』南々社、2007年 * 橘田正徳「はたして難波津はどこにあったのか？」（中世土器研究会『中世土器研究No.126』2010年） --> == 脚注 == <references/> == 関連項目 == * [[難波津 (和歌)]] * [[大阪]] * [[摂津国]] * [[住吉津]] * [[住吉大社]] * [[津守氏]] * [[渡辺津]] * [[渡辺氏]] * [[みおつくし]] * [[八軒家船着場]] * [[四天王寺ワッソ]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:なにわつ}} [[Category:大阪市中央区の歴史]] [[Category:飛鳥時代]] [[Category:奈良時代]] [[Category:摂津国]] [[Category:日本の歴史的港湾]] [[Category:瀬戸内海の交通史]] [[Category:大阪府の交通史]]

{{複数の問題|独自研究=2013年4月|出典の明記=2013年4月|正確性=2013年4月}} '''難波津'''（なにわつ、[[旧仮名遣い|旧]]：なにはつ）は、[[古代]]の[[大阪湾]]に存在したと考えられている古代の[[港湾施設]]である。[[日本書紀]]にその存在が示されているが、2024年現在発見されていない。[[大阪市]][[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]付近に位置していたと考えられている。
== 概要 == [[瀬戸内海]]が現在のような形になったのは[[縄文時代]]のことであるが、当初の[[海岸#海岸線|海岸線]]は[[生駒山]]の[[麓]]、現在の[[東大阪市]]まで入り込んでいた。この湾を[[河内湾]]と呼ぶ。河内湾の入り口に南から突き出ていたのが現在で言う[[上町台地]]である。
その後、河内湾の入り口は[[堆積]]した[[土砂]]で埋まり数条の[[砂州]]・[[砂堆]]となり（吹田砂堆・天満砂堆・難波砂堆）、[[2世紀]]から[[3世紀]]にかけて、河内湾は完全に瀬戸内海から切り離されて「草香江」と呼ばれる[[湖]]となった（[[河内湖]]）。[[古墳時代]]に入ると、人々は[[水運]]の利便性を考えて瀬戸内海と河内湖の間に[[運河]]を掘削し、これを[[難波の堀江|難波堀江]]（なにわのほりえ）と名付けた。河内湖の最奥部の生駒山麓には草香津と呼ばれる港湾施設があり、瀬戸内海から難波堀江を通過して河内湖に入った船は、そのまま東進して草香津に向かった。また難波堀江の途中の、砂州と砂州の間にできていた[[ラグーン|潟湖]]にも港湾施設が建設された。これが難波津である。
また難波津の東、上町台地の先端からは16棟もの[[倉庫]]群の[[遺構]]が[[発掘]]されている。なお、文献史料には「難波館（なにわのむろつみ）」と呼ばれる商館の存在も示されているが、こちらの遺構はいまだ発見されていない。
[[大化の改新]]の後、[[仁徳天皇]]の宮である難波高津宮があったとされる現在の[[法円坂]]周辺に[[遷都|都が戻り]]、[[難波長柄豊碕宮]]（前期[[難波宮]]）が造営された。しかし[[654年]]に[[孝徳天皇]]が[[崩御]]すると都は明日香（現・[[明日香村]]付近）に戻り、[[686年]]には難波長柄豊碕宮は焼失してしまう。[[奈良時代]]にはいわゆる後期難波宮が再建され、一時期[[聖武天皇]]が都を置いた。
ところが、[[8世紀]]に入ると、難波津は土砂の堆積によって港湾施設としての機能を失っていくことになる。[[762年]]に[[安芸国]]から[[回航]]された[[遣唐使]]船が難波津で動けなくなる事件が発生する（『[[続日本紀]]』[[天平宝字]]6年4月丙寅条）など、大型船の停泊が困難になっていった。このため、淀川と三国川（現在の[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]）を結び付ける工事が行われる事になり、その工事は[[785年]]に完成している（『続日本紀』[[延暦]]4年正月庚戌条）。この工事の完成直前の[[784年]]に古来から都を置いていた[[大和国]]を離れて山背国[[長岡京]]に遷都し、同時に難波宮が廃止されて施設が解体されて長岡京に移されているが、その背景として難波津の港湾機能の喪失で外港を失った大和国（飛鳥・[[平城京]]など）では[[西国]]との海上交通に支障をきたしたために、新たな海上交通路を瀬戸内海に注ぐようになった[[淀川]]流域に求めたもので、これによって難波宮も存在意義を失ったとする説もある<ref>北村優季「長岡平安遷都の史的背景」（初出:『国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告』134集（2007年）/所収:北村『平城京成立史論』（吉川弘文館、2013年） ISBN 978-4-642-04610-7）</ref>。これに対して、難波津において[[平安時代]]に入っても難波堀川に生えた草木の伐採（『[[続日本後紀]]』[[承和 (日本)|承和]]12年9月癸亥条）などを行って港湾維持のための努力が続けられていることや『[[土佐日記]]』において[[紀貫之]]が難波を経由したり[[藤原道長]]が[[金剛峯寺]]の帰途に[[四天王寺]]に立ち寄って（摂津）[[国府]]大渡から船に乗って帰洛（『[[扶桑略記]]』[[治安 (日本)|治安]]12年9月癸亥条、なお『続日本後紀』承和11年10月戊子条によれば難波の鴻臚館が廃されて摂津国府に転用されている）したりしていること、平安時代以降も難波津に関する和歌が盛んに詠まれていること（[[#難波津を詠んだ歌|「難波津を詠んだ歌」節参照]]）、そして何よりも難波津衰退説では中世の[[渡辺津]]や[[大江御厨]]の成立が説明できないとして、平安時代に難波宮の廃止などの政治的な変化はあったものの、難波津ではむしろ民間を含めた水上交通が盛んになったとする説もある<ref>西本昌弘「平安時代の難波津と難波宮」（続日本紀研究会編『続日本紀と古代社会』（塙書房、2014年） ISBN 978-4-8273-1271-3）</ref>。
なお、[[中世]]以降の港湾施設については[[渡辺津]]を参照のこと。
== 難波津を詠んだ歌 == * '''難波津に咲くやこの花冬ごもり今は春べと咲くやこの花'''（[[王仁]]） * '''難波潟みじかき葦の節の間も会はでこの世をすごしてよとや'''（[[伊勢 (歌人)|伊勢]]） * '''侘びぬれば今はたおなじ難波なるみをつくしてもあはむとぞ思ふ'''（[[元良親王]]） * '''難波江の葦のかりねの一夜ゆゑ身を尽くしてや恋渡るべき'''（[[皇嘉門院別当]]）
== 難波津の位置 == 難波津が、具体的に現在の[[大阪市]]のどのあたりに位置していたのか、長い間論争が続いている。現在では、有力なものとして[[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]三津寺町付近とする[[千田稔 (歴史地理学者)|千田稔]]の説と、同じく中央区[[高麗橋]]付近とする[[日下雅義]]の説がある。前者の三津寺説は、同寺にまつわる伝承や「三津寺」「堀江」などの名称に根拠を置くものであるが、今のところ、現在の[[心斎橋筋]]付近で古代の港湾遺構が発見されていないのが弱点となっている。他方、高麗橋説については[[考古学]]的な傍証の例が豊富で、近年では、上町台地北端部の西斜面から麓にかけて、[[古墳時代]]から[[奈良時代]]、さらに[[室町時代]]に至るさまざまな時代の港湾関係の遺構が集中的に見つかっていることから、上町台地北端部西麓にあたる現在の[[東横堀川]]・高麗橋周辺が、歴史上の難波津として最有力な地点ではないかと広く考えられるようになっている。
== その後の難波津 == 上述のように、[[桓武天皇]]が[[長岡京]]遷都にあたって[[難波宮|後期難波宮]]の建築物を収去してしまい、更に[[平安京]]遷都が行われると、[[首都]]と[[瀬戸内海]]航路を結ぶ舟運ルートの主力は、平安京との交通至便なより北側を通る[[淀川]]右岸や[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]流域に移行することとなり、[[奈良盆地]]の外港として栄えた淀川左岸の難波津の意義は相対的に小さくなった。また、「難波（なには、なんば）」という地名自体は、上町台地北端のみではなく、あちこちに見られる一般的な地名でもある。このことから、平安時代には、上町台地北端部ではなく別の地域の港湾が「難波津」と呼ばれていたのではないか、更に飛躍して、そもそも古代以来の難波津は現在の大阪市とは別のところに比定されるはずだと主張する説もごく一部に存在するようである。
<!--ところが[[2010年]]（[[平成]]22年）になって、以下の史料解釈を根拠に難波津の位置を豊中市南部に求める新説が提示された。
『日本書紀』推古十六年（608年）四月・六月条の「（前略）為唐客 更造新館於難波高麗館之上、六月壬寅朔丙辰、客等泊于難波津。是日、以飾船卅艘、迎客于江口、安置新館（以下、略）」から、難波津と難波館は江口（難波江口）を挟んだ位置関係にある。「難波高麗館」（難波館）などの位置は、まだ考古学的に確認されていないが、この地域における政治的中枢といえる難波宮の周辺にあったと想定できる。この想定に立つと、難波津は上町台地から難波江口を挟んだ位置となる。なお、難波江口は、『続日本紀』天平宝字六年（762）四月丙寅条「丙寅。遣唐使駕船一隻安藝國到于難波江口。著灘不浮。（以下、略）」などの史料から、難波江の沖合と解せる。
次に、「難波江」は「難波浦」・「難波潟」と同じ地形を示すことから、これらは同じ地域を示す名称と見なせる。その一方で、「難波浦」の位置については『住吉大社神代記』（年不詳・8世紀前半頃？）に「從三國川尻至吾君川尻難波浦」・「爰三韓國調貢從此川運進。」とある。また、『百練抄』と『栄華物語』には、後三条院たちが岩清水社・住吉社・四天王寺を周遊した時の記事がある。このうち、『百練抄』延久五年（1073年）二月条には「（前略）廿二日 覧難波浦。廿五日 覧長柄橋。（以下、略）」と、また『栄華物語』「松のしづえ」延久五年（1073年）条では、（二月）廿二日に長柄橋から中津川（現在の淀川）へ、そして廿五日には御幣島にかけて周遊したとある。御幣島は、現在の大阪市西淀川区御幣島のことであるから、少なくとも「難波浦」（＝難波江）とは三国川河口一帯すなわち現在の神崎川下流域に比定される。
以上から、難波津は上町台地から三国川河口の沖合を挟んだ位置、つまり大阪市淀川区・豊中市・尼崎市南部一帯がその候補地となる。
さらに、遣唐使船の出発地を抽出すると「難波江口（江口）」、「三津」、「難波津」の3カ所が挙げられる。「三津」については、『続日本紀』天平勝宝五年（753年）九月条の「摂津國御津村南風大吹。」の「御津村」と混同されるが、遣唐使船あるいは外国使節船との関係で現れる地名に「御津」の表記はない。よって、「三津（浦・浜）」と「御津」は区別でき、前者は大阪市淀川区三津屋付近となる可能性があることから、大阪市側に難波津の位置を求める根拠は乏しい。また、天平勝宝8年（756年）に作成された『摂津職河辺郡猪名所地図』に「大物濱」「長洲濱」「杭瀬濱」という海浜にかかる記述があるにもかかわらず、「難波津」はみられないので、尼崎市域も候補地から除外される。残る豊中市南部については、三国川分流と考えられる2条以上の旧河道が確認されているが、その一つは現在の上津島・名神口付近で古代の猪名川と合流すると推定される。その合流点付近には、弥生時代から平安時代にかけての流通拠点と位置づけられている利倉南遺跡・利倉西遺跡・上津島遺跡・上津島南遺跡・島田遺跡・庄内遺跡（以後、これらの遺跡を「上津島遺跡群」と呼ぶ。）が展開している。
以上、各候補地の状況をふまえると、難波津の位置は豊中市南部の上津島・名神口に展開する上津島遺跡群に求めるのが、最も合理的である。

{{複数の問題|独自研究=2013年4月|出典の明記=2013年4月|正確性=2013年4月}} '''難波津'''（なにわつ、[[旧仮名遣い|旧]]：なにはつ）は、[[古代]]の[[大阪湾]]に存在したと考えられている古代の[[港湾施設]]である。[[日本書紀]]にその存在が示されているが、2024年現在遺構は発見されていない。[[大阪市]][[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]付近に位置していたと考えられている。
== 概要 == [[瀬戸内海]]が現在のような形になったのは[[縄文時代]]のことであるが、当初の[[海岸#海岸線|海岸線]]は[[生駒山]]の[[麓]]、現在の[[東大阪市]]まで入り込んでいた。この湾を[[河内湾]]と呼ぶ。河内湾の入り口に南から突き出ていたのが現在で言う[[上町台地]]である。
その後、河内湾の入り口は[[堆積]]した[[土砂]]で埋まり数条の[[砂州]]・[[砂堆]]となり（吹田砂堆・天満砂堆・難波砂堆）、[[2世紀]]から[[3世紀]]にかけて、河内湾は完全に瀬戸内海から切り離されて「草香江」と呼ばれる[[湖]]となった（[[河内湖]]）。[[古墳時代]]に入ると、人々は[[水運]]の利便性を考えて瀬戸内海と河内湖の間に[[運河]]を掘削し、これを[[難波の堀江|難波堀江]]（なにわのほりえ）と名付けた。河内湖の最奥部の生駒山麓には草香津と呼ばれる港湾施設があり、瀬戸内海から難波堀江を通過して河内湖に入った船は、そのまま東進して草香津に向かった。また難波堀江の途中の、砂州と砂州の間にできていた[[ラグーン|潟湖]]にも港湾施設が建設された。これが難波津である。
また難波津の東、上町台地の先端からは16棟もの[[倉庫]]群の[[遺構]]が[[発掘]]されている。なお、文献史料には「難波館（なにわのむろつみ）」と呼ばれる商館の存在も示されているが、こちらの遺構はいまだ発見されていない。
[[大化の改新]]の後、[[仁徳天皇]]の宮である難波高津宮があったとされる現在の[[法円坂]]周辺に[[遷都|都が戻り]]、[[難波長柄豊碕宮]]（前期[[難波宮]]）が造営された。しかし[[654年]]に[[孝徳天皇]]が[[崩御]]すると都は明日香（現・[[明日香村]]付近）に戻り、[[686年]]には難波長柄豊碕宮は焼失してしまう。[[奈良時代]]にはいわゆる後期難波宮が再建され、一時期[[聖武天皇]]が都を置いた。
ところが、[[8世紀]]に入ると、難波津は土砂の堆積によって港湾施設としての機能を失っていくことになる。[[762年]]に[[安芸国]]から[[回航]]された[[遣唐使]]船が難波津で動けなくなる事件が発生する（『[[続日本紀]]』[[天平宝字]]6年4月丙寅条）など、大型船の停泊が困難になっていった。このため、淀川と三国川（現在の[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]）を結び付ける工事が行われる事になり、その工事は[[785年]]に完成している（『続日本紀』[[延暦]]4年正月庚戌条）。この工事の完成直前の[[784年]]に古来から都を置いていた[[大和国]]を離れて山背国[[長岡京]]に遷都し、同時に難波宮が廃止されて施設が解体されて長岡京に移されているが、その背景として難波津の港湾機能の喪失で外港を失った大和国（飛鳥・[[平城京]]など）では[[西国]]との海上交通に支障をきたしたために、新たな海上交通路を瀬戸内海に注ぐようになった[[淀川]]流域に求めたもので、これによって難波宮も存在意義を失ったとする説もある<ref>北村優季「長岡平安遷都の史的背景」（初出:『国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告』134集（2007年）/所収:北村『平城京成立史論』（吉川弘文館、2013年） ISBN 978-4-642-04610-7）</ref>。これに対して、難波津において[[平安時代]]に入っても難波堀川に生えた草木の伐採（『[[続日本後紀]]』[[承和 (日本)|承和]]12年9月癸亥条）などを行って港湾維持のための努力が続けられていることや『[[土佐日記]]』において[[紀貫之]]が難波を経由したり[[藤原道長]]が[[金剛峯寺]]の帰途に[[四天王寺]]に立ち寄って（摂津）[[国府]]大渡から船に乗って帰洛（『[[扶桑略記]]』[[治安 (日本)|治安]]12年9月癸亥条、なお『続日本後紀』承和11年10月戊子条によれば難波の鴻臚館が廃されて摂津国府に転用されている）したりしていること、平安時代以降も難波津に関する和歌が盛んに詠まれていること（[[#難波津を詠んだ歌|「難波津を詠んだ歌」節参照]]）、そして何よりも難波津衰退説では中世の[[渡辺津]]や[[大江御厨]]の成立が説明できないとして、平安時代に難波宮の廃止などの政治的な変化はあったものの、難波津ではむしろ民間を含めた水上交通が盛んになったとする説もある<ref>西本昌弘「平安時代の難波津と難波宮」（続日本紀研究会編『続日本紀と古代社会』（塙書房、2014年） ISBN 978-4-8273-1271-3）</ref>。
なお、[[中世]]以降の港湾施設については[[渡辺津]]を参照のこと。
== 難波津を詠んだ歌 == * '''難波津に咲くやこの花冬ごもり今は春べと咲くやこの花'''（[[王仁]]） * '''難波潟みじかき葦の節の間も会はでこの世をすごしてよとや'''（[[伊勢 (歌人)|伊勢]]） * '''侘びぬれば今はたおなじ難波なるみをつくしてもあはむとぞ思ふ'''（[[元良親王]]） * '''難波江の葦のかりねの一夜ゆゑ身を尽くしてや恋渡るべき'''（[[皇嘉門院別当]]）
== 難波津の位置 == 難波津が、具体的に現在の[[大阪市]]のどのあたりに位置していたのか、長い間論争が続いている。現在では、有力なものとして[[中央区 (大阪市)|中央区]]三津寺町付近とする[[千田稔 (歴史地理学者)|千田稔]]の説と、同じく中央区[[高麗橋]]付近とする[[日下雅義]]の説がある。前者の三津寺説は、同寺にまつわる伝承や「三津寺」「堀江」などの名称に根拠を置くものであるが、今のところ、現在の[[心斎橋筋]]付近で古代の港湾遺構が発見されていないのが弱点となっている。他方、高麗橋説については[[考古学]]的な傍証の例が豊富で、近年では、上町台地北端部の西斜面から麓にかけて、[[古墳時代]]から[[奈良時代]]、さらに[[室町時代]]に至るさまざまな時代の港湾関係の遺構が集中的に見つかっていることから、上町台地北端部西麓にあたる現在の[[東横堀川]]・高麗橋周辺が、歴史上の難波津として最有力な地点ではないかと広く考えられるようになっている。
== その後の難波津 == 上述のように、[[桓武天皇]]が[[長岡京]]遷都にあたって[[難波宮|後期難波宮]]の建築物を収去してしまい、更に[[平安京]]遷都が行われると、[[首都]]と[[瀬戸内海]]航路を結ぶ舟運ルートの主力は、平安京との交通至便なより北側を通る[[淀川]]右岸や[[神崎川 (大阪府・兵庫県)|神崎川]]流域に移行することとなり、[[奈良盆地]]の外港として栄えた淀川左岸の難波津の意義は相対的に小さくなった。また、「難波（なには、なんば）」という地名自体は、上町台地北端のみではなく、あちこちに見られる一般的な地名でもある。このことから、平安時代には、上町台地北端部ではなく別の地域の港湾が「難波津」と呼ばれていたのではないか、更に飛躍して、そもそも古代以来の難波津は現在の大阪市とは別のところに比定されるはずだと主張する説もごく一部に存在するようである。
<!--ところが[[2010年]]（[[平成]]22年）になって、以下の史料解釈を根拠に難波津の位置を豊中市南部に求める新説が提示された。
『日本書紀』推古十六年（608年）四月・六月条の「（前略）為唐客 更造新館於難波高麗館之上、六月壬寅朔丙辰、客等泊于難波津。是日、以飾船卅艘、迎客于江口、安置新館（以下、略）」から、難波津と難波館は江口（難波江口）を挟んだ位置関係にある。「難波高麗館」（難波館）などの位置は、まだ考古学的に確認されていないが、この地域における政治的中枢といえる難波宮の周辺にあったと想定できる。この想定に立つと、難波津は上町台地から難波江口を挟んだ位置となる。なお、難波江口は、『続日本紀』天平宝字六年（762）四月丙寅条「丙寅。遣唐使駕船一隻安藝國到于難波江口。著灘不浮。（以下、略）」などの史料から、難波江の沖合と解せる。
次に、「難波江」は「難波浦」・「難波潟」と同じ地形を示すことから、これらは同じ地域を示す名称と見なせる。その一方で、「難波浦」の位置については『住吉大社神代記』（年不詳・8世紀前半頃？）に「從三國川尻至吾君川尻難波浦」・「爰三韓國調貢從此川運進。」とある。また、『百練抄』と『栄華物語』には、後三条院たちが岩清水社・住吉社・四天王寺を周遊した時の記事がある。このうち、『百練抄』延久五年（1073年）二月条には「（前略）廿二日 覧難波浦。廿五日 覧長柄橋。（以下、略）」と、また『栄華物語』「松のしづえ」延久五年（1073年）条では、（二月）廿二日に長柄橋から中津川（現在の淀川）へ、そして廿五日には御幣島にかけて周遊したとある。御幣島は、現在の大阪市西淀川区御幣島のことであるから、少なくとも「難波浦」（＝難波江）とは三国川河口一帯すなわち現在の神崎川下流域に比定される。
以上から、難波津は上町台地から三国川河口の沖合を挟んだ位置、つまり大阪市淀川区・豊中市・尼崎市南部一帯がその候補地となる。
さらに、遣唐使船の出発地を抽出すると「難波江口（江口）」、「三津」、「難波津」の3カ所が挙げられる。「三津」については、『続日本紀』天平勝宝五年（753年）九月条の「摂津國御津村南風大吹。」の「御津村」と混同されるが、遣唐使船あるいは外国使節船との関係で現れる地名に「御津」の表記はない。よって、「三津（浦・浜）」と「御津」は区別でき、前者は大阪市淀川区三津屋付近となる可能性があることから、大阪市側に難波津の位置を求める根拠は乏しい。また、天平勝宝8年（756年）に作成された『摂津職河辺郡猪名所地図』に「大物濱」「長洲濱」「杭瀬濱」という海浜にかかる記述があるにもかかわらず、「難波津」はみられないので、尼崎市域も候補地から除外される。残る豊中市南部については、三国川分流と考えられる2条以上の旧河道が確認されているが、その一つは現在の上津島・名神口付近で古代の猪名川と合流すると推定される。その合流点付近には、弥生時代から平安時代にかけての流通拠点と位置づけられている利倉南遺跡・利倉西遺跡・上津島遺跡・上津島南遺跡・島田遺跡・庄内遺跡（以後、これらの遺跡を「上津島遺跡群」と呼ぶ。）が展開している。
以上、各候補地の状況をふまえると、難波津の位置は豊中市南部の上津島・名神口に展開する上津島遺跡群に求めるのが、最も合理的である。
== 関連文献 == * 北川建次ほか編『瀬戸内海事典』南々社、2007年 * 橘田正徳「はたして難波津はどこにあったのか？」（中世土器研究会『中世土器研究No.126』2010年） --> == 脚注 == <references/> == 関連項目 == * [[難波津 (和歌)]] * [[大阪]] * [[摂津国]] * [[住吉津]] * [[住吉大社]] * [[津守氏]] * [[渡辺津]] * [[渡辺氏]] * [[みおつくし]] * [[八軒家船着場]] * [[四天王寺ワッソ]]

[[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Naniwazu-qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:redirect]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

[[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Naniwazu-qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:redirect]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.
== See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:qq]] <!--Naniwazu-->[[Category:redirect]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:redirect]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.
== See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:qq]] <!--Naniwazu-->[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].

== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.

{{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:History of Chūō-ku, Osaka]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Nara period]] [[Category:Settsu Province]] [[Category:Historical ports in Japan]] [[Category:Maritime history of the Seto Inland Sea]] [[Category:Transportation history of Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:goodbye $1 most likely untranslated or similar]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:goodbye $1 most likely untranslated or similar]] [[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Naniwazu}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]]

[[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] <!--Naniwazu-->[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.
== See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

[[Category:11]][[sn:Naniwazu]][[Category:11]] <!--Naniwazu-->[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Naniwazu]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:難波津]] '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu.== See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.

Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. '''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.

Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)

Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

'''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].

Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.

Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.

In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].

Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

revision.text:
'''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in [[Osaka Bay]] during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day [[Chūō-ku, Osaka|Chūō Ward, Osaka City]].
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now [[Higashiōsaka]]. This inlet was known as [[Kawachi Bay]]. The [[Uemachi Plateau]] protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, [[Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace]] (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the [[Nara period]], the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from [[Aki Province]] became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the [[Yodo River]] and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the [[Heian period]] to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]

Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the [[Muromachi period]], leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * [[Osaka]] * [[Settsu Province]] * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]
