revision.text:
'''Naniwa-tsu''' (難波津, "Port of Naniwa") was an ancient port facility believed to have existed in {{ill|Osaka Bay|en|Osaka Bay|WD=Q999162|lt=Osaka Bay|1=Osaka Bay}} during antiquity. Although its presence is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]], no archaeological remains have been discovered as of 2024. It is thought to have been located in the present-day {{ill|Chūō-ku, Osaka|en|Chūō-ku, Osaka|WD=Q876485|lt=Chūō Ward, Osaka City|1=Chūō-ku, Osaka}}.
== Overview == The [[Seto Inland Sea]] took its present form during the [[Jōmon period]], but its coastline originally extended to the foothills of [[Ikoma Mountains]], reaching what is now {{ill|Higashiōsaka|en|Higashiōsaka|WD=Q243863|lt=Higashiōsaka|1=Higashiōsaka}}. This inlet was known as {{ill|Kawachi Bay|en|Kawachi Bay|WD=Q11553429|lt=Kawachi Bay|1=Kawachi Bay}}. The {{ill|Uemachi Plateau|en|Uemachi Plateau|WD=Q7877450|lt=Uemachi Plateau|1=Uemachi Plateau}} protruded from the south, marking the entrance of Kawachi Bay.
Over time, sedimentation led to the formation of sandbanks and ridges (Suita Ridge, Tenma Ridge, Naniwa Ridge), completely isolating Kawachi Bay from the Seto Inland Sea by the 2nd to 3rd centuries. The enclosed body of water became known as "[[Kusakae]] Lake" (later referred to as [[Kawachi Lake]]). During the [[Kofun period]], people excavated a canal connecting the lake to the sea, known as the [[Naniwa no Horie]] (Naniwa Canal). Ships traveling from the Seto Inland Sea would pass through this canal to reach Kusakae Lake, where the [[Kusakae-tsu]] port was located. A separate port, Naniwa-tsu, was constructed along the lagoon formed between the sandbanks.
Excavations on the eastern edge of the Uemachi Plateau have revealed the remains of 16 storage warehouses. Historical records also reference a commercial warehouse district known as "Naniwa-no-Murotsumi," though its archaeological remains have yet to be discovered.
== Historical Development == Following the [[:en:Taika Reforms|Taika Reforms]], the capital was temporarily relocated near the present-day [[Hōenzaka]] area, where the [[Naniwa-Takatsu Palace]] was established. Subsequently, {{ill|Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|en|Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|WD=Q11659570|lt=Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace|1=Naniwa Nagara-Toyosaki Palace}} (also called "Early Naniwa Palace") was constructed. However, after the death of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] in 654, the capital was moved back to {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}}, and in 686, Naniwa Palace was destroyed by fire. During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the "Later Naniwa Palace" was rebuilt, and Emperor [[Shōmu]] briefly established his court there.
By the 8th century, Naniwa-tsu had begun to lose its functionality due to sediment accumulation. In 762, an [[envoy to Tang China]] ship from {{ill|Aki Province|en|Aki Province|WD=Q965029|lt=Aki Province|1=Aki Province}} became stranded in the harbor ("Shoku Nihongi," Tenpyō-Hōji 6, 4th month, 3rd day). By 785, construction was completed to connect the {{ill|Yodo River|en|Yodo River|WD=Q1140505|lt=Yodo River|1=Yodo River}} and [[Mikuni River]] (now known as [[ Kanzaki River (Osaka, Hyōgo) | Kanzaki River]]), forming an alternative transportation route. Shortly before this, in 784, the capital was moved to [[Nagaoka-kyō]], leading to the abandonment and dismantling of Naniwa Palace. Some scholars argue that the decline of Naniwa-tsu as a viable port was a factor in the relocation, as it impeded maritime trade with the western regions.
== Later Developments == Despite its decline as an official port, efforts continued into the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} to maintain water routes, including vegetation clearance along Naniwa Canal ("Shoku Nihon Kōki," Jōwa 12, 9th month). Even in later periods, the port remained a notable feature in literature, as seen in works such as the "[[:en:Tosa Diary|Tosa Diary]]" and references to Naniwa in waka poetry. Some scholars challenge the notion of complete decline, arguing that commercial shipping remained active, and the development of medieval ports like [[Watanabe-tsu]] and [[Ōe Mikuriya]] suggests the continuation of waterborne trade.
For medieval harbor facilities, see [[Watanabe-tsu]].
== Poetry about Naniwa-tsu == * "Naniwa-tsu ni / saku ya kono hana / fuyu gomori / ima wa haru be to / saku ya kono hana" (Wani) * "Naniwa-gata / mijikaki ashi no / fushi no ma mo / awade kono yo o / sugoshite yo to ya" (Ise) * "Wabi nureba / ima wa ta onaji / Naniwa naru / mi o tsukushite mo / awan to zo omou" (Motoyoshi Shinnō) * "Naniwa-e no / ashi no karine no / hitoyo yue / mi o tsukushite ya / koi watarubeki" (Kōkamon'in Bettō)
== Possible Locations of Naniwa-tsu == The exact location of Naniwa-tsu remains a subject of scholarly debate. Prominent theories suggest sites near present-day [[Mitsuteramachi]] (advocated by [[Chida Minoru]]) or [[:en:Koraibashi|Koraibashi]] (proposed by [[Kusaka Masayoshi]]). The Mitsuteramachi theory draws upon local traditions and place names, though no archaeological remains have been found in the [[Shinsaibashi-suji]] area. The Koraibashi theory is supported by a substantial number of excavated port-related structures dating from the [[Kofun period]] through the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, leading to a consensus that the western slope of the northern Uemachi Plateau, near present-day [[Tōyoko-borigawa]], was likely the site of the historical Naniwa-tsu. == See Also == * [[Naniwa-tsu (Waka)]] * {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} * {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}} * [[Sumiyoshi-tsu]] * [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] * [[Tsumori clan]] * [[Watanabe-tsu]] * [[:en:Watanabe clan|Watanabe clan]] * [[Mio-tsukushi]] * [[Hakkenya Boat Landing]] * [[Shitennō-ji Wasso]]
